A History of Mind

Antiquity Late Antiquity South Asian East Asian Islamic Jewish Scholasticism
Renaissance Early Modern
19th Century Phenomenology Analytic
Consciousness Science Machine Minds Moral Status
Glossary About
Early Modern

George Berkeley

1685 – 1753

Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as an active-spirit theory.

Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is best reconstructed as an active-spirit theory. Its most basic distinction is not mind versus body, but spirit versus idea. Ideas are passive objects of awareness: colors, sounds, tastes, pains, tactile qualities, imagined scenes, remembered appearances, and the ordered complexes we call ordinary bodies. Spirits are active perceivers and agents: the self, other human minds, possible non-human created minds, and God as infinite spirit. Berkeley’s famous immaterialism is therefore not the claim that only “my ideas” exist. It is a much stronger metaphysics: ordinary objects are real as divinely ordered ideas, but there is no mind-independent material substance underlying them. Reality, for human knowledge, is exhausted by ideas and spirits. Berkeley says explicitly that besides ideas there is “something which knows or perceives them,” exercises operations such as willing, imagining, and remembering, and is called “mind,” “spirit,” “soul,” or “myself.”

The mind is not an idea among ideas. This is decisive. For Berkeley, a soul or spirit cannot be pictured, sensed, or represented by an idea, because ideas are passive and a spirit is active. A spirit is “simple,” “undivided,” and “active”; as perceiving ideas it is called understanding, and as producing or operating on ideas it is called will. The mind is therefore not a bundle of mental contents, not a brain-process, not a material substance, and not an abstract support hidden behind experience. It is the active subject of perceiving, willing, imagining, remembering, reasoning, and attending.

The mind of God is not a pious appendage to this system. It is one of the load-bearing parts of Berkeley’s philosophy of mind. God is the infinite spirit whose will accounts for the involuntary, vivid, coherent, lawlike order of sensory ideas. Without God, Berkeley would have great difficulty explaining object permanence, natural law, intersubjective reality, and the difference between real perception and private fancy. His philosophy of mind is therefore theistic at its metaphysical core, not merely in its apologetic packaging.

Angels receive much less explicit treatment in the core works than God or human minds, but they matter philosophically. Berkeley’s ontology allows, in principle, for finite created spirits other than human beings. Such beings would not be ideas; they would be active perceivers, knowable only by their operations or signs, and subordinate to God’s infinite mind. Angelic minds therefore form an important test case for Berkeley’s philosophy: they show that “mind” in Berkeley is not essentially brain-bound, human-bodied, or empirically restricted to our sensory apparatus, even though human knowledge of such minds would be indirect and analogical.

2. Why the system must be reconstructed rather than merely summarized

Berkeley did not leave a complete treatise called “On Mind.” His published early masterpieces—An Essay towards a New Theory of Vision (1709), A Treatise concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge (1710), and Three Dialogues between Hylas and Philonous (1713)—contain the materials for a philosophy of mind, but not a fully developed psychology, philosophy of action, theory of animal consciousness, angelology, or theology of divine cognition. The Internet Encyclopedia of Philosophy notes that Berkeley’s projected continuation of the Principles, expected to address mind, God, morality, and freedom more fully, was lost; the 1734 editions then added the doctrine of “notions” to help explain how we understand mind and spirit without having an idea of them.

This incompleteness should not be mistaken for poverty. The core materials are unusually rich: an ontology of spirits and ideas, a theory of self-knowledge, a theory of other minds, an account of divine cognition, a semiotic theory of perception, an anti-mechanist account of the brain, a theory of science as natural signs rather than material efficient causes, and a view of the soul’s natural immortality. Modern Cambridge scholarship is right to caution that Berkeley says “too little” on many philosophy-of-mind questions and that his treatment of causation and animal minds is programmatic rather than fully systematic.

The right procedure, then, is to extract a Berkeleyan philosophy of mind from his ontology, epistemology, theory of perception, theology, and philosophy of science, while marking where the extraction is secure and where it becomes interpretive.

3. Berkeley’s basic ontology: ideas and spirits

Berkeley begins from what is immediately known. The objects of human knowledge are ideas: sensory ideas, ideas of imagination and memory, and affective or reflective contents. But ideas alone are insufficient. There must also be a perceiver or knower of them. The perceiver is not another idea; it is the active being Berkeley calls mind, spirit, soul, or self.

The mature ontology has two fundamental kinds of being. First, ideas: passive, inert, dependent, perceivable items. Second, spirits: active, perceiving, willing beings. Berkeley thus abolishes mind–matter dualism but replaces it with a different, sharper dualism: spirit–idea dualism. The Stanford Encyclopedia of Philosophy describes this as a dualism inside Berkeley’s immaterialist world: ideas are passive, spirits active, and God is an infinite spirit. Talia Mae Bettcher’s interpretation, in a Spanish-language SciELO venue, makes the point more forcefully: Berkeley’s spirits and ideas are “entirely distinct,” not simply two species within a single scale of reality; ideas are not modes or accidents inhering in spirit in the ordinary scholastic sense.

This matters for philosophy of mind because Berkeley is not saying that bodies are “inside the mind” as spatial contents. He explicitly rejects the inference that, because extension and color exist only in mind, the mind itself must be extended or colored. Sensible qualities are “in” the mind only as perceived, not as attributes modifying the mind the way a shape modifies a body.

The formula “to be is to be perceived” is therefore incomplete unless paired with a complementary principle for spirits: to be a spirit is not to be perceived, but to perceive. The German Metzler Lexikon Philosophie captures this well: Berkeley’s esse est percipi applies to experiential objects, but must be complemented by esse est percipere for subjects. Being is divided into ideas and spirits.

4. What a mind or soul is

Berkeley’s soul is not a hidden material object, not an immaterial image, and not an inferred fiction. It is the active subject of mental life. In the Principles, he defines spirit through its powers: it thinks, wills, and perceives. “Will,” “understanding,” “mind,” “soul,” and “spirit” do not name separate ideas; they mark aspects of an active being that cannot itself be represented by any idea.

The mind has at least two principal dimensions. As understanding, it receives or perceives ideas. As will, it produces, orders, combines, or operates on ideas. This distinction is not always perfectly clean, because Berkeley also treats attention, judgment, and selective consideration as activities of mind. Spanish-language work in Philosophica emphasizes this tension: Berkeley’s understanding can look passive insofar as it receives sense ideas, yet the mind’s selective attention and consideration introduce activity even into cognition.

The soul is simple and indivisible. This simplicity grounds Berkeley’s argument for its natural immortality. Since bodies are passive complexes of ideas, bodily motions, corruptions, and dissolutions cannot by themselves destroy an active, simple, unextended spirit. Berkeley is careful: the soul is not absolutely incapable of annihilation by God, but it is not naturally dissoluble by bodily change.

This gives Berkeley a distinctive anti-reductionist theory of mind. The mind is not reducible to the body because bodies are ideas or complexes of ideas. It is not reducible to a brain because the brain, too, is an idea-complex in experience. It is not reducible to a passive stream of perceptions because perceptions require an active subject. It is not known by an image because it is not imageable. It is known by what Berkeley calls a notion.

5. Ideas, notions, and the problem of knowing the self

The distinction between idea and notion is crucial. We have ideas of colors, sounds, shapes, pains, and imagined objects. We do not have an idea of the soul, because no passive image can resemble an active subject. Yet Berkeley insists that words such as “soul,” “spirit,” “will,” and “mind” are not meaningless. We understand them notionally: by inward awareness of our own activity and by understanding the operations of willing, loving, hating, perceiving, and reasoning.

This is Berkeley’s answer to a powerful objection: if we reject matter because we have no idea of it, why not reject spirit for the same reason? Berkeley’s reply is that the cases are asymmetrical. Matter, as an unperceiving support of sensible qualities, is incoherent or empty. Spirit, by contrast, is not an object of sense but is directly involved in every act of perceiving and willing. We do not know spirit as we know a triangle; we know it as the active “I” that perceives and acts.

Berkeley’s early notebooks briefly toy with a more Hume-like formulation, calling mind a “congeries of perceptions,” but the mature Principles moves away from that. The editor of Berkeley’s works explicitly notes this development: the later position makes mind “entirely distinct” from ideas or perceptions. This development is philosophically important. Berkeley’s mature self is not a bundle; it is an active unity that owns, orders, and operates upon perceptions.

The self is therefore immediately known, but not as an object. In the Three Dialogues, Berkeley says the soul is indivisible and unextended, and that he knows himself immediately or intuitively. He also says that the notion of God is formed by reflecting on one’s own soul, heightening its powers, and removing imperfections. Spanish-language scholarship on Berkeley’s knowledge of God develops this point: finite spirits know themselves as active, perceiving, thinking, acting principles, and from this self-knowledge gain an inadequate but meaningful route toward understanding God as infinite spirit.

6. The soul always thinks: Berkeley on time and mental duration

Berkeley’s theory of time is also a theory of mind. He rejects abstract time conceived apart from the succession of ideas. Time, for a finite spirit, is measured by the succession of ideas or actions in that spirit. Hence, Berkeley concludes that the soul “always thinks,” because to abstract the existence of a spirit from its cogitation is unintelligible.

This does not mean that every mental act is reflective or self-conscious in a modern explicit sense. It means that a finite spirit’s duration is not a material clock-time existing independently of all experience. Its lived duration is constituted by the ordered succession of its perceptions and acts. In Berkeleyan terms, the soul is not first a bare substance that then occasionally receives thoughts; to be a soul is to be an active perceiver whose existence is inseparable from thinking, willing, or perceiving.

7. Perception: the mind as receiver, interpreter, and learner of signs

Berkeley’s theory of perception is one of the most sophisticated parts of his philosophy of mind. In New Theory of Vision, he argues that distance is not immediately seen. Distance is not a visible datum but is learned through associations between visual ideas and tactile/motor expectations. Sight does not deliver a geometrical map of external space by unconscious calculation; it gives visual signs that, by habit and divine ordering, suggest what tactile ideas would follow from action.

The proper objects of sight, Berkeley says, are light and colors with their variations. Visual ideas do not resemble tangible ideas; they signify or suggest them. Thus vision is a kind of natural language. Berkeley concludes that the proper objects of vision constitute a “Universal Language of Nature,” guiding action toward bodily preservation and well-being.

This gives Berkeley a proto-semiotic philosophy of perception. The mind is not a passive screen receiving pictures of external material objects. It learns to read sensory signs. Visual experience is practical, anticipatory, and action-guiding. The Italian OpenEdition article on Berkeley’s aesthetic relation to nature emphasizes this point: visual ideas function as signs of tactile ideas, their connection is not necessary but instituted by God, and the visual order becomes a divine language through which human beings orient themselves in nature.

This also affects Berkeley’s account of objectivity. Real perception is not private fantasy because sensory ideas arrive involuntarily, vividly, coherently, and according to stable laws. The order of sense is not constructed by my will. It is given by another spirit, ultimately the divine Author of nature.

8. Imagination, memory, and voluntary mental production

Berkeley distinguishes sensory ideas from ideas of imagination. I can voluntarily call up, alter, combine, or dismiss ideas in fancy. This voluntary production is a central mark of mental activity. In contrast, when I open my eyes in daylight, I cannot simply choose what sensory objects will appear. Sense ideas therefore reveal a source other than my own finite will.

The difference between reality and imagination lies not in matter but in order, vividness, steadiness, coherence, and dependence on will. Ideas imprinted by the Author of nature are “real things”; imagined ideas are less regular, vivid, and constant. This is one of Berkeley’s most important contributions to philosophy of mind: he relocates the real/imaginary distinction inside experience without reducing reality to personal whim.

Dreams, hallucinations, and illusions create pressure here. Berkeley’s answer is not that real things are material while dreams are mental. Both are ideas. The difference is that waking sensory experience belongs to a coherent, public, law-governed order that resists individual volition and connects with action. Dreams are comparatively irregular, confused, and disconnected from the stable divine order. This answer is plausible within his system, though it remains one of the areas where critics can press him.

9. The body and the brain: Berkeley’s anti-neuromaterialism

Berkeley’s philosophy of mind dissolves the classical mind–body problem by denying material substance. The body is not a material machine containing or producing the soul. It is a stable complex of sensible ideas ordered by God. My own body is a privileged complex within experience: its visible and tactile changes are lawfully connected with my volitions, actions, pleasures, pains, and practical orientation.

Berkeley is especially sharp against brain-based explanations of perception. In the Three Dialogues, Hylas appeals to motions in the brain and nerves to explain sensations. Philonous replies that if the brain is perceived or imagined, it is itself an idea; explaining one idea by another idea in the brain does not produce a genuine material cause. If the brain is not perceived or conceivable, then it is unintelligible. Berkeley also denies that any clear connection is available between nerve-motion and the sensation of color or sound.

This is not an empirical denial that brain-events correlate with experience. Berkeley can accept lawful correlations among perceived bodily signs. What he rejects is the metaphysical claim that an unperceived material brain efficiently produces consciousness. On Berkeley’s strict view, the brain belongs to the order of ideas; it can be part of the natural sign-system, but not the ultimate cause of mind.

10. Human agency and the will

Berkeley’s mind is active above all in willing. The will is not a motion of a material soul; it is not a passive effect mechanically necessitated by sensory objects. Berkeley warns that metaphors drawn from sensible motion distort the soul, as when philosophers imagine the will as a ball pushed by external objects.

What exactly finite spirits cause is contested. At minimum, Berkeley clearly holds that finite minds produce volitions and ideas of imagination. He also often writes as though we move our bodies by willing their motions. His notebooks state that “we move our legs ourselves” and that this distinguishes him from Malebranche. Yet modern interpreters disagree about how far this goes. Sukjae Lee argues that Berkeley does not hold that finite spirits causally contribute to bodily motion; on that reading, our volitions are occasions for God to cause bodily movement, while our own activity consists in producing volitions and ideas of imagination.

A balanced reconstruction should therefore distinguish secure Berkeleyan agency from contested Berkeleyan agency. Securely, finite spirits will, attend, imagine, remember, combine, and reason. Securely, the mind is not passive matter. Less securely, finite spirits may or may not be efficient causes of bodily motion in a robust sense. If Berkeley is a concurrentist, God and finite spirits cooperate in action; if he is closer to occasionalism, God produces bodily motions in response to finite volitions. The texts leave room for debate.

11. Other minds

Berkeley’s treatment of other minds is indirect but important. We do not perceive other spirits immediately. We perceive ideas—bodily motions, words, gestures, patterns of behavior—and infer active minds from them. Berkeley says other spirits are known by their operations, or by the ideas they excite in us.

This is parallel to the knowledge of God, but with a difference of scale. A finite assemblage of signs denotes another human mind; the whole order of nature denotes God. Berkeley even argues that God is in one sense more evident than other human minds, because the signs of divine activity are everywhere and constant, whereas the signs of particular human minds are limited.

The result is a sign-based theory of social cognition. I do not see another soul as an object; I read a meaningful order of signs. Human communication, bodily expression, and language become ways in which finite spirits disclose themselves within the divine sensory order. The 2024 volume Berkeley’s Doctrine of Signs describes signs as a unifying theme across Berkeley’s philosophy, extending through perception, language, epistemology, mathematics, science, and theology.

12. Animal minds

Berkeley’s published philosophy does not give a full theory of animal consciousness. He occasionally refers to animal instincts, appetites, and passions as part of the natural order that testifies to divine wisdom, but he does not systematically ask whether animals reason, whether they are spirits in the same sense as humans, or how animal subjectivity should be understood.

A Berkeleyan extension is possible. If animals genuinely perceive, will, suffer, and act, they would seem to be finite spirits or at least centers of perception. If they are mere complexes of ideas, then animal pain and agency become difficult to respect. Berkeley does not settle this in the core texts. For a comprehensive Berkeleyan philosophy of mind, animal minds are therefore an open but unavoidable problem.

13. Angels and non-human created spirits

Berkeley’s core immaterialist texts do not provide a developed angelology. That absence matters, but it should not lead us to dismiss angels as irrelevant. Berkeley’s ontology is not restricted to human minds. In the Three Dialogues, Philonous considers “all finite created spirits” and then argues that beyond them there must be an omnipresent eternal Mind that knows and comprehends all things.

Within Berkeley’s framework, angels would be finite created spirits. They would not be material bodies, and they would not be ideas. They would be active perceivers and agents, knowable—if at all—through signs, operations, or effects. We would not have an idea of an angelic mind any more than we have an idea of our own mind or God’s mind. We could only form a notion by analogy with our own active spirit, while removing human bodily limitations only cautiously.

Angels also expose an important issue in Berkeley’s theory of embodiment. Human perceptual life is deeply tied to visual, tactile, motor, and bodily signs. A pure or angelic spirit would not necessarily have our visual-tactile sign system. Berkeley’s New Theory of Vision even discusses the difficulty of entering into the thought of a “pure spirit” with respect to geometry, because human beings struggle to disentangle purely visible objects from tactile associations. Thus angelic minds are philosophically significant because they test whether Berkeley’s theory of mind is essentially sensory-human or more broadly spiritual.

Angels cannot replace God in Berkeley’s system. A finite angelic mind might perceive some objects, communicate with some minds, or serve as an instrument within providence. But only an infinite, omnipresent, eternal mind can secure the universal continuity and lawlike order of nature. So angels fit Berkeley’s ontology as created spirits, but the metaphysical role of world-sustainer belongs to God alone.

14. The mind of God

Berkeley’s God is an infinite spirit, not an impersonal law, not a material world-soul, and not a mere name for the regularity of nature. God has will and understanding; he is the cause of the orderly ideas of sense; he sustains object permanence; and he communicates through the natural order. The Stanford Encyclopedia summarizes Berkeley’s argument by elimination: sensory ideas are not caused by other ideas, not by matter, and not by my own will, so they must be caused by another spirit; given their complexity, coherence, and order, that spirit is God.

Berkeley’s God is not known by having an idea of God. Like every spirit, God cannot be represented by a passive idea. God is known by reflection, analogy, signs, and reason. In the Three Dialogues, Philonous says that by reflecting on his own soul and heightening its powers while removing imperfections, he gains a notion—not an idea—of God. Spanish scholarship on Berkeley’s “three ways” to knowledge of God develops this internal route: the finite soul, as an imperfect image of God, provides a reflective path to understanding divine activity, freedom, agency, and action.

God is also known through the world as a sign-system. Berkeley rejects the Malebranchean doctrine that we see things by seeing representations in God’s essence. He says instead that we perceive sensible signs and infer their divine cause, just as we infer human spirits from signs, though the divine signs are universal and continuous.

A delicate issue is whether God has ideas. Berkeley says sensible things continue to exist because they are known and comprehended by the infinite mind. Yet he also denies that God has sensations in the passive, suffering, creaturely way we do. God knows pain and can cause painful sensations in us, but God does not suffer pain. God is pure spirit, not a finite subject overwhelmed by involuntary sensory impressions.

The Three Dialogues introduces a useful distinction between two “states” of things: an archetypal, eternal state in the mind of God and an ectypal or natural state in created time. This is not a fully worked-out theory, but it helps avoid the crude picture that ordinary objects are simply private sensations in God’s head. The divine mind is the eternal ground, rule, and source of the sensible order, while finite minds receive temporally ordered ideas according to divine laws.

Recent Acta Philosophica work by Alberto Luis López usefully identifies four attributes of Berkeley’s God: volitional, creator-upholder, perceptive, and permanent interlocutor. This captures God’s philosophy-of-mind role: God is not merely the first cause of the world but an infinite communicative intelligence whose ordered signs make finite cognition possible.

15. Natural law and science: signs, not material efficient causes

Berkeley is often misread as anti-scientific. He is not. He rejects material efficient causes, but he preserves natural philosophy as the study of orderly phenomena. Scientists discover analogies, harmonies, rules, and regularities among ideas. They do not discover hidden material powers; they learn the grammar of nature.

Berkeley says philosophers err when they look for natural efficient causes distinct from mind or spirit. Laws of nature are not mechanical necessities inhering in matter; they are regularities in the divine production of ideas. The value of science is predictive, practical, and contemplative: it lets us anticipate future experiences, coordinate action, and admire the order of creation.

This is a philosophy of mind because it makes cognition fundamentally interpretive. To know nature is to read signs. Berkeley compares scientific reasoning to reading a book: wise readers attend to sense and use, not merely grammar. The mind’s relation to nature is therefore hermeneutic as well as perceptual.

16. Language, meaning, and mental content

Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of language. He rejects the assumption that every meaningful word must stand for an idea. This is crucial because otherwise words such as “spirit,” “will,” “soul,” “God,” “virtue,” and “danger” would be meaningless whenever no distinct image accompanies them. Berkeley notes that words can immediately stir emotions, guide conduct, and function meaningfully even when they do not raise a determinate idea in the hearer.

This linguistic point rescues talk of mind from a narrow image-theory of meaning. If meaning required a mental picture, then “soul” and “God” would fail. But Berkeley’s mature doctrine allows meaningful discourse through notions, signs, practical use, and affective or rational orientation. Treccani’s Italian entry makes this point in connection with Alciphron: Berkeley’s later account of language helps prevent “I” and “spirit” from becoming empty terms by treating them through an operative and pragmatic conception of signs.

17. Object permanence and the mind of God

The standard objection is simple: if to be is to be perceived, does the tree vanish when no human looks at it? Berkeley’s answer is not that the tree exists in matter. It exists because it is perceivable under stable laws and, more strongly in the Dialogues, because it is comprehended by the infinite mind of God. When sensible things continue after my annihilation, they must exist in another mind; all finite created spirits considered, there is an omnipresent eternal mind that knows and exhibits things according to the laws of nature.

This makes God central to Berkeley’s philosophy of mind. The world is not dependent on any one finite consciousness. It is dependent on mind as such, ultimately divine mind. Berkeley is therefore not a solipsist. He is a theistic immaterial realist: ordinary objects are real, public, and stable, but their reality is grounded in perception and divine ordering, not in material substrate.

18. Late Berkeley and Siris: aether, life, and cosmic spirit

Berkeley’s late work Siris complicates the picture. It moves through tar-water, chemistry, medicine, ancient philosophy, light, aether, life, and Neoplatonic metaphysics. It should not be treated as an embarrassing theological or medical curiosity. It shows Berkeley wrestling with life, embodiment, and mediation between divine agency and the living world.

Treccani characterizes the late Siris phase as moving toward a scale of natural essences, Stoic-Neoplatonic motifs, the Timaeus, and a more explicit language of a spirit pervading and shaping natural reality. A Cairn.info study of Siris argues that the work is not extemporaneous but addresses the major philosophical problem of the principle of life; Berkeley’s answer centers on an invisible aethereal spirit—fire, anima mundi, and substance of light—understood as the instrument of the incorporeal Agent.

This late “spirit” language must be handled carefully. It is not identical with “spirit” in the strict Principles sense of active mind or soul. In Siris, “spirit” can also mean subtle aether, light, fire, animal spirit, or mediating life-principle. Airaksinen’s article on Siris describes the work as deliberately ambiguous between scientific-material and metaphysical-immaterial registers, subordinating mechanisms to the superiority of minds or agents.

For a philosophy of mind, Siris is valuable because it reopens the question of embodiment and life. The early Berkeley can sound as though bodies are simply passive idea-complexes. The late Berkeley becomes more interested in living mediation, vitality, and the relation between microcosm and macrocosm. This does not clearly overturn immaterialism, but it expands Berkeley’s vocabulary for thinking about embodied life under divine agency.

19. Multilingual reception: what non-Anglophone sources add

Italian reception often stresses Berkeley’s spiritual realism and Platonizing dimension. Treccani describes him as a subtle interpreter of the modern “way of ideas” who pushes it toward immaterialism in order to defeat materialistic science and reconcile philosophy with common sense; it also characterizes the result as a new Platonism centered on the primacy of spirits, especially God. This is valuable because it resists the narrow Anglophone habit of reading Berkeley only as an empiricist predecessor of Hume.

Spanish-language scholarship is especially helpful on God and spirit. Philosophica emphasizes that God is not merely a theological ornament: the figure of God is philosophically indispensable, but Berkeley’s system cannot be reduced to simple apologetics. God is the infinite spirit whose will, perception, and sustaining activity explain the structure and meaning of the world. The Cairn article “Berkeley: tres vías para el conocimiento de dios” develops the internal, reflective route from self-knowledge to divine knowledge, including the idea that finite spirits know God through their own active spiritual nature, though inadequately.

French sources highlight the anti-material-substance argument. An Érudit article explains that, for Berkeley, if sensible ideas exist only in being perceived, then a non-spiritual matter could not be the substrate of sensible qualities, because matter by definition cannot perceive them. It also stresses Berkeley’s principle that an idea can resemble only another idea, not an imperceptible material object. Older French reception also wrestled with whether Berkeley is better classified as empiricist or spiritualist, precisely because his empiricism about ideas is inseparable from his spiritual ontology.

German lexicographical reception usefully frames Berkeley’s ontology as the conjunction of esse est percipi for ideas and esse est percipere for subjects, and it explicitly connects the theological motive to the rejection of autonomous matter standing beside God. This helps clarify why Berkeley does not merely deny matter but replaces it with dependence on mind.

Portuguese reception, represented by Vanderlei de Oliveira Farias’s article “Berkeley e a Realidade Mental do Mundo,” frames Berkeley as developing a new conception of reality that blocks skeptical questions about the external world and remains relevant to current philosophy of mind. A Costa Rican/Spanish decolonial reading by Gustavo Hernández-Castro is more critical: it argues that Berkeley’s empiricism, grounded in a philosophical-theological structure, contributes to abstract universalism and a problematic treatment of subjectivity. That critique should not be swallowed uncritically, but it is useful: it reminds us that Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is also a philosophy of subjecthood, authority, universality, and divine order.

20. Reconstructed Berkeleyan philosophy of mind

A comprehensive Berkeleyan philosophy of mind can be stated as follows.

First, the mind is an active spiritual substance, not a material thing, not a brain, and not a passive bundle. It is simple, unextended, indivisible, and known notionally rather than imagistically. Its core powers are perceiving, willing, thinking, imagining, remembering, attending, and reasoning.

Second, mental contents are ideas, but ideas are not self-subsistent. They depend on being perceived by some mind. Ordinary bodies are stable, law-governed complexes of sensible ideas, not material substances hidden behind appearances. This does not abolish ordinary reality; Berkeley repeatedly insists that houses, trees, rivers, stars, and bodies remain real in the common-sense meaning of “real.”

Third, the self is known immediately but not as an object. Berkeley rejects the demand for an idea of the self. The self is the active “I” disclosed in willing, perceiving, and thinking. This gives him a non-representational account of self-knowledge: self-awareness is not inner observation of a mental object but practical-intellectual acquaintance with one’s own agency.

Fourth, perception is semiotic and practical. The senses deliver signs. Vision, especially, is a learned divine language by which visible ideas suggest tactile and motor possibilities. Human perception is therefore action-guiding, anticipatory, embodied, and learned, while still grounded in divine order.

Fifth, the real/imaginary distinction is intra-mental but objective. Real things are not material; they are sensory ideas impressed according to stable divine laws. Imaginations are more voluntary, faint, irregular, and private. This allows Berkeley to resist both materialism and solipsism.

Sixth, causation is fundamentally spiritual. Ideas do not cause ideas. True efficient causation belongs to spirits. Finite spirits clearly cause volitions and imaginative ideas; whether they efficiently cause bodily motions is debated. God causes the ordered sensory world.

Seventh, the body is a privileged idea-complex, not the producer of mind. The brain can be a lawful sign within experience, but it cannot be the metaphysical cause of consciousness. Berkeley’s anti-neuromaterialism follows directly from his claim that brains, nerves, and motions are themselves perceived ideas.

Eighth, other minds are known by signs. Human bodies, speech, gestures, and actions signify finite spirits. God is known similarly but more universally: the whole order of nature is a sign of divine mind.

Ninth, God is the infinite mind whose will sustains the natural order. God is not a passive sensorium and does not suffer sensations as finite minds do. God knows all, causes the sensory order, and communicates through nature.

Tenth, angels and other non-human spirits are possible finite created minds within the system. Berkeley does not develop their psychology, but his ontology can accommodate them as active perceivers distinct from ideas. They are philosophically important because they show that Berkeley’s concept of spirit is broader than human embodied consciousness, though only God can play the role of universal sustainer.

Eleventh, the soul is naturally immortal. Because it is simple, active, unextended, and not a body, it cannot be dissolved by bodily decay or natural motion. Its continued existence still depends on God, so Berkeley’s immortality claim is natural rather than absolute.

Twelfth, Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is inseparable from moral and theological anthropology. Mind is not a neutral computational system; it is a finite spiritual agent living under divine order, capable of error, freedom, moral action, religious knowledge, and relation to God.

21. Major difficulties and open questions

The first major difficulty is the spirit/matter asymmetry problem. Berkeley rejects matter partly because we have no idea of it, but we also have no idea of spirit. His answer is the notion/idea distinction. This answer is powerful, but not cost-free. Critics can ask whether “notion” gives enough positive content to spirit, or whether it merely shields spirit from the same critique applied to matter.

The second difficulty is the divine ideas problem. If objects persist because God perceives them, does God have sensory ideas? Berkeley denies that God senses or suffers as we do, but he also says sensible things are known and comprehended by God. The archetype/ectype distinction helps, but does not fully dissolve the tension.

The third difficulty is finite agency. Berkeley wants minds to be active, yet sense ideas are involuntary and bodily motions may be divinely produced. If finite minds cause too little, Berkeley risks occasionalism; if they cause too much, he must explain how immaterial finite spirits affect the publicly ordered world. Modern scholarship remains divided here.

The fourth difficulty is other minds. Berkeley’s sign-based inference to other minds is elegant, but it makes interpersonal knowledge indirect. Since bodies are ideas, the signs of another mind are not material bodies but divinely ordered idea-complexes. This can work, but it depends on the stability and reliability of the divine sign-system.

The fifth difficulty is animals and angels. Berkeley’s ontology has room for non-human finite spirits, but his published system gives no detailed taxonomy of created minds. A complete Berkeleyan philosophy of mind would need to ask whether animals are spirits, whether angels have sensory ideas, whether disembodied finite spirits perceive space, and whether non-human spirits participate in the same natural sign-language as human beings.

The sixth difficulty is Siris and continuity. Late Berkeley’s aether, fire, animal spirit, and world-soul language can look like a partial return of subtle matter or cosmic mediation. A charitable reading treats this as a layered vocabulary of divine instruments and life-principles, not a simple abandonment of immaterialism. Still, Siris complicates the clean spirit/idea dualism of the early works.

22. Final assessment

Berkeley’s philosophy of mind is a radical alternative to materialism, Cartesian dualism, representationalism, and bundle theories of the self. Its governing idea is that the real agents in the world are not bodies but spirits. The mind is active, simple, unextended, and notionally known; ideas are passive, dependent, and perceivable; bodies are ordered idea-complexes; science studies the grammar of divine signs; other minds are known through meaningful operations; God is the infinite communicative mind sustaining the entire order of experience.

The mind of God is indispensable, not decorative. Angels, though not systematically treated, are legitimate philosophical possibilities within the ontology of created spirits and help reveal the breadth of Berkeley’s concept of mind. Berkeley’s system is therefore not merely “subjective idealism.” It is a theistic, semiotic, anti-materialist philosophy of active spiritual agency. Its deepest ambition is to show that reality is not less intelligible when matter is removed, but more intelligible: the world becomes a meaningful order of ideas sustained by mind, addressed to minds, and ultimately grounded in the infinite mind of God.

Digital Minds · A history of thinking about the mind, from antiquity to the present day.
A personal research project by Fabián. · About · Glossary
